title: Docker Setup for PostgreSQL
description: Complete guide to setting up PostgreSQL using Docker on Windows - includes both Docker Desktop and Hyper-V alternatives

concepts.title: Installing Docker Desktop

sqlServer.code:
-- SQL Server Installation - Traditional Setup
-- Requires SQL Server installer from Microsoft
-- Full installation with default instance:

-- 1. Run SQL Server Setup.exe
-- 2. Select "Installation" -> "New SQL Server standalone installation"
-- 3. Choose edition (Developer/Express for free)
-- 4. Feature Selection: Database Engine Services
-- 5. Instance Configuration: Default instance (MSSQLSERVER)
-- 6. Service Accounts: Use defaults or specify accounts
-- 7. Authentication: Mixed Mode recommended
-- 8. Data Directories: Accept defaults or customize

-- Verification query after installation:
SELECT @@VERSION;
SELECT SERVERPROPERTY('ServerName') AS ServerName,
       SERVERPROPERTY('InstanceName') AS InstanceName,
       SERVERPROPERTY('Edition') AS Edition;
sqlServer.description: SQL Server installation is a traditional Windows installer process that requires downloading substantial installation media (several GB) and configures Windows services directly. The installation creates system databases, configures network protocols, and sets up the SQL Server service to start automatically. Requires local administrator privileges. Installation size: 2-4GB+ depending on features. Creates Windows services: MSSQLSERVER, SQLSERVERAGENT. Includes SQL Server Configuration Manager for service management.

postgres.code:
# PostgreSQL with Docker Desktop - Modern Container Setup

# 1. Download and Install Docker Desktop for Windows
# Visit: https://www.docker.com/get-started/ and download docker desktop

# 2. System Requirements Check
# - Windows 10/11 Pro, Enterprise, or Education (WSL2 backend)
# - OR Windows 10/11 Home with WSL2 enabled
# - 4GB RAM minimum, 8GB recommended
# - Virtualization enabled in BIOS/UEFI

# 3. Installation Steps:
# - Run Docker Desktop Installer
# - Enable WSL2 backend (recommended) or Hyper-V
# - Complete installation and restart

# 4. Verify Docker Installation
docker --version
docker-compose --version

# 5. Pull and Run PostgreSQL Container
docker run --name postgres-dev \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=YourStrongPassword123! \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=testdb \
  -p 5432:5432 \
  -v postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  -d postgres:15

# 6. Verify PostgreSQL is running
docker ps
docker logs postgres-dev

# 7. Connect to PostgreSQL
docker exec -it postgres-dev psql -U postgres -d testdb
postgres.description: Docker Desktop provides a lightweight, containerized approach to running PostgreSQL. Unlike traditional installation, containers are isolated, easily replaceable, and don't modify your host system. The entire PostgreSQL instance runs in a container with persistent data storage through Docker volumes. Container starts in seconds vs minutes for traditional installation. Data persists through postgres-data volume even if container is deleted. Easy to run multiple PostgreSQL versions simultaneously. Uses ~100-200MB RAM vs SQL Server's 1GB+ minimum.

concepts.keyDifference: Container isolation vs direct Windows service installation. Instant setup/teardown vs permanent system modification. Version management through image tags vs manual upgrades. Portable configuration vs Windows registry settings.

concepts.title: WSL2 vs Hyper-V Backend Choice

sqlServer.code:
-- SQL Server Virtualization Options
-- Traditional approaches for running SQL Server in virtual environments

-- Option 1: Hyper-V Virtual Machine
-- - Full Windows Server VM with SQL Server installed
-- - Best compatibility, highest resource usage
-- - Typical configuration:
--   * Windows Server 2019/2022
--   * 4-8GB RAM allocated to VM
--   * 50-100GB virtual disk
--   * SQL Server installed normally within VM

-- Option 2: Windows Containers (Limited Support)
-- SQL Server 2017+ supports Windows containers
-- Requires Windows Server containers mode
docker run -e "ACCEPT_EULA=Y" -e "SA_PASSWORD=YourPassword123!" \
       -p 1433:1433 --name sql1 \
       -d mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server:2019-latest

-- Verification inside VM or container:
SELECT @@VERSION;
SELECT SERVERPROPERTY('ComputerNamePhysicalNetBIOS') AS ComputerName;
sqlServer.description: SQL Server virtualization traditionally relies on full VMs using Hyper-V, VMware, or similar hypervisors. Each VM runs a complete Windows Server instance with SQL Server installed normally. Windows containers for SQL Server are available but less commonly used and require Windows Server container mode. Full VM approach provides complete isolation and compatibility. High resource overhead: VM OS + SQL Server + Windows services. Traditional licensing applies to virtual cores/instances. Backup/restore works exactly as on physical machines.

postgres.code:
# Docker Desktop Backend Comparison for PostgreSQL

# WSL2 Backend (Recommended for most users)
# ================================================
# Advantages:
# - Better performance for Linux containers
# - Lower resource usage
# - Faster file I/O for bind mounts
# - Native Linux environment for containers

# Check current backend:
docker context ls
docker info | findstr "Operating System"

# WSL2 Performance Test:
docker run --rm postgres:15 \
  sh -c "echo 'Performance test: WSL2 backend'"

# Hyper-V Backend (Legacy, Windows Containers)
# =============================================
# Advantages:
# - Better isolation between containers and host
# - Required for Windows containers
# - More familiar to Windows administrators

# Switch backend in Docker Desktop Settings:
# Settings > General > Use the WSL2 based engine

# Performance Comparison Example:
# Start PostgreSQL with performance monitoring
docker run --name postgres-perf-test \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=testpass \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=perftest \
  -p 5432:5432 \
  -v postgres-perf:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  -d postgres:15

# Test database performance
docker exec -it postgres-perf-test psql -U postgres -d perftest -c "\
  CREATE TABLE performance_test (id SERIAL, data TEXT); \
  INSERT INTO performance_test (data) \
  SELECT 'test data ' || generate_series(1,10000); \
  \timing on \
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM performance_test;"
postgres.description: Docker Desktop offers two backends: WSL2 (recommended) and Hyper-V (legacy). WSL2 provides better performance for Linux containers like PostgreSQL because it runs containers in a lightweight Linux kernel rather than a full VM. WSL2 typically offers 20-50% better performance for database workloads. WSL2 is default and recommended for PostgreSQL containers. Hyper-V backend required only for Windows container support. WSL2 provides faster startup times and better resource efficiency. Both backends support the same Docker commands and features.

concepts.keyDifference: WSL2: Linux kernel integration vs Hyper-V: Full virtualization layer. WSL2: ~100MB overhead vs Hyper-V: ~1GB+ VM overhead. WSL2: Native Linux filesystem vs Hyper-V: Windows filesystem translation. WSL2: Better Docker development experience vs Hyper-V: Better enterprise isolation.

concepts.title: Creating PostgreSQL Container

sqlServer.description: SQL Server database creation involves connecting to an existing SQL Server instance and using CREATE DATABASE statements to define database files, initial sizes, growth settings, and recovery models. User creation requires separate LOGIN (server-level) and USER (database-level) objects with role-based permissions. Requires existing SQL Server instance to be running. Database files (.mdf/.ldf) created on local filesystem. File paths must be accessible to SQL Server service account. Recovery model affects transaction log behavior.

postgres.code:
# PostgreSQL Container Creation - Complete Setup

# Basic PostgreSQL Container
docker run --name myapp-postgres \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=myappdb \
  -e POSTGRES_USER=app_user \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=SecurePassword123! \
  -p 5432:5432 \
  -v myapp-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  -d postgres:15

# Advanced Configuration with Custom Settings
docker run --name myapp-postgres-advanced \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=myappdb \
  -e POSTGRES_USER=app_user \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=SecurePassword123! \
  -e POSTGRES_INITDB_ARGS="--encoding=UTF8 --locale=en_US.UTF-8" \
  -p 5432:5432 \
  -v myapp-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  -v myapp-config:/etc/postgresql/postgresql.conf \
  --shm-size=256MB \
  -d postgres:15 \
  -c max_connections=200 \
  -c shared_buffers=256MB \
  -c effective_cache_size=1GB

# Verify container is running
docker ps -f name=myapp-postgres

# Check container logs
docker logs myapp-postgres

# Connect to the database
docker exec -it myapp-postgres psql -U app_user -d myappdb

# Alternative: Connect from host using psql client
# psql -h localhost -p 5432 -U app_user -d myappdb

# Create additional database and user within container
docker exec -it myapp-postgres psql -U postgres -c "\
  CREATE DATABASE testdb OWNER app_user; \
  CREATE USER read_user WITH PASSWORD 'ReadOnlyPass123!'; \
  GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE testdb TO read_user; \
  \c testdb \
  GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO read_user; \
  GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO read_user; \
  ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA public GRANT SELECT ON TABLES TO read_user;"

# Container management commands
docker start myapp-postgres     # Start existing container
docker stop myapp-postgres      # Stop container
docker restart myapp-postgres   # Restart container
docker remove myapp-postgres    # Delete container (data persists in volume)
postgres.description: PostgreSQL container creation is accomplished with a single Docker command that includes environment variables for database initialization, port mapping for connectivity, and volume mounting for data persistence. The container includes a complete PostgreSQL installation and automatically initializes the database on first startup. Container creates database, user, and schema automatically on first run. Data persists in Docker volume even if container is deleted. Multiple containers can run simultaneously on different ports. Configuration changes can be made through command-line parameters or config files.

concepts.keyDifference: Single Docker command vs multiple T-SQL statements. Environment variables vs explicit CREATE statements. Automatic initialization vs manual database creation. Container lifecycle management vs Windows service management.

concepts.title: Data Persistence with Volumes

sqlServer.code:
-- SQL Server Data Persistence - File-based Storage
-- Data files are directly managed by SQL Server on Windows filesystem

-- 1. Check current database file locations
SELECT
    name AS LogicalName,
    physical_name AS PhysicalPath,
    size * 8 / 1024 AS SizeMB,
    max_size * 8 / 1024 AS MaxSizeMB
FROM sys.database_files;

-- 2. Database files persist automatically
-- Default locations:
-- Data: C:\Program Files\Microsoft SQL Server\MSSQL15.MSSQLSERVER\MSSQL\DATA\
-- Logs: C:\Program Files\Microsoft SQL Server\MSSQL15.MSSQLSERVER\MSSQL\DATA\

-- 3. Backup for data protection
BACKUP DATABASE MyAppDB
TO DISK = 'C:\Backups\MyAppDB_Full.bak'
WITH COMPRESSION, CHECKSUM, INIT;

-- 4. Restore database to recover data
RESTORE DATABASE MyAppDB_Restored
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\MyAppDB_Full.bak'
WITH MOVE 'MyAppDB' TO 'C:\Program Files\Microsoft SQL Server\MSSQL15.MSSQLSERVER\MSSQL\DATA\MyAppDB_Restored.mdf',
     MOVE 'MyAppDB_Log' TO 'C:\Program Files\Microsoft SQL Server\MSSQL15.MSSQLSERVER\MSSQL\DATA\MyAppDB_Restored_Log.ldf',
     REPLACE;

-- 5. Detach/Attach for moving databases
-- Detach (removes database from SQL Server but keeps files)
USE master;
ALTER DATABASE MyAppDB SET SINGLE_USER WITH ROLLBACK IMMEDIATE;
EXEC sp_detach_db 'MyAppDB';

-- Attach (registers existing database files with SQL Server)
CREATE DATABASE MyAppDB ON
(FILENAME = 'C:\NewLocation\MyAppDB.mdf'),
(FILENAME = 'C:\NewLocation\MyAppDB_Log.ldf')
FOR ATTACH;
sqlServer.description: SQL Server data persistence is handled through .mdf (data) and .ldf (log) files stored directly on the Windows filesystem. These files are managed by the SQL Server service and persist automatically as long as the files remain on disk. Database backup/restore and detach/attach operations provide data portability. Data files (.mdf) and log files (.ldf) are created automatically. Files persist even if SQL Server service stops. File locations are configurable during database creation. Backup/restore provides point-in-time recovery capabilities.

postgres.code:
# PostgreSQL Data Persistence with Docker Volumes

# 1. Create named volume for persistent storage
docker volume create postgres-app-data
docker volume create postgres-config
docker volume create postgres-backups

# 2. List and inspect volumes
docker volume ls
docker volume inspect postgres-app-data

# 3. Create PostgreSQL container with persistent volumes
docker run --name postgres-persistent \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=myappdb \
  -e POSTGRES_USER=app_user \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=SecurePassword123! \
  -p 5432:5432 \
  -v postgres-app-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  -v postgres-config:/etc/postgresql \
  -v postgres-backups:/backups \
  -d postgres:15

# 4. Test data persistence
# Connect and create test data
docker exec -it postgres-persistent psql -U app_user -d myappdb -c "\
  CREATE TABLE test_persistence (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, created TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()); \
  INSERT INTO test_persistence (name) VALUES ('Test Record 1'), ('Test Record 2'), ('Test Record 3'); \
  SELECT * FROM test_persistence;"

# 5. Stop and remove container (data remains in volume)
docker stop postgres-persistent
docker rm postgres-persistent

# 6. Create new container using same volume
docker run --name postgres-restored \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=myappdb \
  -e POSTGRES_USER=app_user \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=SecurePassword123! \
  -p 5432:5432 \
  -v postgres-app-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  -d postgres:15

# 7. Verify data persistence
docker exec -it postgres-restored psql -U app_user -d myappdb -c "\
  SELECT * FROM test_persistence;"

# 8. Backup volume data
docker run --rm \
  -v postgres-app-data:/source \
  -v postgres-backups:/backup \
  alpine tar czf /backup/postgres-data-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).tar.gz -C /source .

# 9. Restore volume from backup
docker run --rm \
  -v postgres-app-data:/target \
  -v postgres-backups:/backup \
  alpine sh -c "cd /target && tar xzf /backup/postgres-data-20231201-143000.tar.gz"

# 10. Volume cleanup (WARNING: Destroys data)
# docker volume rm postgres-app-data postgres-config postgres-backups
postgres.description: Docker volumes provide persistent storage that exists independently of container lifecycle. When a PostgreSQL container is deleted, the volume containing database data remains intact and can be attached to new containers. This separation allows for container updates, configuration changes, and even host migration while preserving data. CRITICAL: Always use volumes for production PostgreSQL containers. Data in volumes persists even when container is deleted. Volumes can be backed up and restored independently. Multiple containers can share the same volume (with care). Volume data is stored in Docker's managed directory.

concepts.keyDifference: Volume-based persistence vs direct file system storage. Container-independent data vs service-dependent files. Portable volumes vs fixed file paths. Container lifecycle separation vs integrated storage.

concepts.title: Connection Management & Tools

sqlServer.code:
-- SQL Server Connection Management
-- Multiple connection methods and tools available

-- 1. SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) - Primary GUI tool
-- Connection details:
-- Server name: localhost or .\MSSQLSERVER (default instance)
-- Authentication: Windows Authentication or SQL Server Authentication
-- Database: master (or specific database)

-- 2. Command-line connection using sqlcmd
sqlcmd -S localhost -E
-- Or with SQL Authentication:
sqlcmd -S localhost -U sa -P YourPassword

-- 3. Connection string examples (for applications)
-- .NET Framework:
-- "Server=localhost;Database=MyAppDB;Integrated Security=true;"
-- "Server=localhost;Database=MyAppDB;User Id=app_user;Password=SecurePass123!;"

-- 4. Test connection from T-SQL
SELECT
    @@SERVERNAME as ServerName,
    SUSER_NAME() as CurrentUser,
    DB_NAME() as CurrentDatabase,
    @@VERSION as Version;

-- 5. View active connections
SELECT
    session_id,
    login_name,
    host_name,
    program_name,
    database_id,
    DB_NAME(database_id) as database_name,
    status,
    cpu_time,
    memory_usage,
    total_scheduled_time,
    login_time,
    last_request_start_time
FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions
WHERE is_user_process = 1;

-- 6. Connection troubleshooting
-- Check if SQL Server is running:
-- Services.msc -> SQL Server (MSSQLSERVER)
-- Check TCP/IP is enabled:
-- SQL Server Configuration Manager -> Protocols for MSSQLSERVER
sqlServer.description: SQL Server connection management centers around SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) as the primary GUI tool, with sqlcmd for command-line access. Connections use either Windows Authentication (integrated security) or SQL Server Authentication (username/password). The SQL Server Browser service helps with instance discovery. SSMS provides comprehensive database management capabilities. Windows Authentication is generally preferred for security. Default port 1433 for default instance. SQL Server Configuration Manager controls network protocols.

postgres.code:
# PostgreSQL Connection Management & Tools

# 1. Command-line connection using psql
# Basic connection to containerized PostgreSQL
docker exec -it postgres-persistent psql -U app_user -d myappdb

# Connection from host (requires psql client installed)
psql -h localhost -p 5432 -U app_user -d myappdb

# Connection with full connection string
psql "postgresql://app_user:SecurePassword123!@localhost:5432/myappdb"

# 2. pgAdmin 4 - Web-based GUI (PostgreSQL's "SSMS equivalent")
# Run pgAdmin in Docker container
docker run --name pgadmin \
  -e PGADMIN_DEFAULT_EMAIL=admin@example.com \
  -e PGADMIN_DEFAULT_PASSWORD=AdminPassword123! \
  -p 8080:80 \
  -v pgadmin-data:/var/lib/pgadmin \
  -d dpage/pgadmin4

# Access pgAdmin at: http://localhost:8080
# Add server in pgAdmin:
# Host: host.docker.internal (if PostgreSQL in another container)
# Host: localhost (if PostgreSQL on host)
# Port: 5432
# Username: app_user
# Password: SecurePassword123!

# 3. Alternative GUI Tools
# DBeaver (Universal database tool)
# - Download from dbeaver.io
# - Connection: PostgreSQL
# - Host: localhost, Port: 5432
# - Database: myappdb, User: app_user

# HeidiSQL (Windows)
# - Supports PostgreSQL in newer versions
# - Similar setup to DBeaver

# 4. Test connection and get database info
docker exec -it postgres-persistent psql -U app_user -d myappdb -c "\
  SELECT version(); \
  \conninfo \
  \l \
  \d"

# 5. View active connections
docker exec -it postgres-persistent psql -U postgres -d postgres -c "\
  SELECT
    pid,
    usename,
    application_name,
    client_addr,
    client_hostname,
    client_port,
    backend_start,
    query_start,
    state,
    query
  FROM pg_stat_activity
  WHERE state = 'active';"

# 6. Connection string examples (for applications)
# .NET Core with Npgsql:
# "Host=localhost;Port=5432;Database=myappdb;Username=app_user;Password=SecurePassword123!"

# Python with psycopg2:
# "postgresql://app_user:SecurePassword123!@localhost:5432/myappdb"

# 7. Connection troubleshooting
# Check container status
docker ps -f name=postgres-persistent
docker logs postgres-persistent --tail 50

# Test network connectivity
telnet localhost 5432
# Or using PowerShell:
Test-NetConnection -ComputerName localhost -Port 5432
postgres.description: PostgreSQL connection management offers multiple tools: psql for command-line access, pgAdmin as the web-based GUI equivalent to SSMS, and various third-party tools like DBeaver. Containerized PostgreSQL requires either exec commands to connect from within the container or proper port mapping for external connections. pgAdmin provides most SSMS-equivalent functionality in a web interface. psql offers more advanced command-line features than sqlcmd. Container networking requires understanding of Docker port mapping. Multiple GUI tools available: pgAdmin, DBeaver, HeidiSQL, etc.

concepts.keyDifference: Web-based pgAdmin vs desktop SSMS application. psql command-line vs sqlcmd. Docker exec for container access vs direct local connection. Connection strings use different formats and parameters.

concepts.title: Daily Container Management

sqlServer.code:
-- SQL Server Daily Management Tasks
-- Using SQL Server Management Studio and T-SQL commands

-- 1. Check SQL Server service status
-- Services.msc -> SQL Server (MSSQLSERVER)
-- Or via PowerShell:
-- Get-Service -Name MSSQLSERVER

-- 2. Start/Stop SQL Server service (PowerShell as Administrator)
-- Start-Service -Name MSSQLSERVER
-- Stop-Service -Name MSSQLSERVER
-- Restart-Service -Name MSSQLSERVER

-- 3. Monitor current activity
SELECT
    session_id,
    login_name,
    program_name,
    host_name,
    status,
    cpu_time,
    memory_usage,
    reads,
    writes,
    login_time,
    last_request_start_time
FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions
WHERE is_user_process = 1
ORDER BY cpu_time DESC;

-- 4. Check database status
SELECT
    name,
    database_id,
    state_desc,
    user_access_desc,
    recovery_model_desc,
    log_reuse_wait_desc
FROM sys.databases
WHERE name NOT IN ('master', 'tempdb', 'model', 'msdb');

-- 5. Monitor disk space
SELECT
    DB_NAME(database_id) AS DatabaseName,
    name AS LogicalName,
    physical_name AS PhysicalName,
    size * 8 / 1024 AS SizeMB,
    max_size * 8 / 1024 AS MaxSizeMB
FROM sys.master_files
ORDER BY database_id;

-- 6. View SQL Server error log
EXEC xp_readerrorlog 0, 1, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, N'desc';

-- 7. Check backup status
SELECT
    d.name AS DatabaseName,
    MAX(b.backup_finish_date) AS LastBackupDate,
    DATEDIFF(hour, MAX(b.backup_finish_date), GETDATE()) AS HoursSinceLastBackup
FROM sys.databases d
LEFT JOIN msdb.dbo.backupset b ON d.name = b.database_name
WHERE d.name NOT IN ('tempdb')
GROUP BY d.name
ORDER BY LastBackupDate;
sqlServer.description: SQL Server daily management involves monitoring Windows services, checking database status through T-SQL queries, and using SSMS for visual monitoring. Service control requires administrator privileges and affects all databases on the instance. Error logs and system views provide operational insights. SQL Server runs as Windows service (starts with system by default). Service control affects entire SQL Server instance. Error log location: Program Files\Microsoft SQL Server\...\LOG. SSMS provides Activity Monitor for real-time monitoring.

postgres.code:
# PostgreSQL Daily Container Management

# 1. Check container status
docker ps -f name=postgres-persistent
docker stats postgres-persistent --no-stream

# 2. Start/Stop/Restart container
docker start postgres-persistent
docker stop postgres-persistent
docker restart postgres-persistent

# 3. View container logs (recent activity)
docker logs postgres-persistent --tail 100 --follow
docker logs postgres-persistent --since "24h"
docker logs postgres-persistent --timestamps

# 4. Monitor PostgreSQL activity
docker exec -it postgres-persistent psql -U postgres -c "\
  SELECT
    pid,
    usename,
    application_name,
    client_addr,
    backend_start,
    query_start,
    state,
    wait_event_type,
    wait_event,
    query
  FROM pg_stat_activity
  WHERE state != 'idle'
  ORDER BY backend_start;"

# 5. Check database sizes and activity
docker exec -it postgres-persistent psql -U postgres -c "\
  SELECT
    datname AS database_name,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) AS size,
    numbackends AS active_connections,
    xact_commit AS transactions_committed,
    xact_rollback AS transactions_rolled_back,
    blks_read + blks_hit AS total_blocks_accessed,
    stats_reset AS last_stats_reset
  FROM pg_stat_database
  WHERE datname NOT IN ('template0', 'template1')
  ORDER BY pg_database_size(datname) DESC;"

# 6. Monitor system resources
docker exec -it postgres-persistent psql -U postgres -c "\
  SELECT
    setting AS shared_buffers,
    unit,
    context,
    short_desc
  FROM pg_settings
  WHERE name = 'shared_buffers';

  SELECT
    name,
    setting,
    unit,
    short_desc
  FROM pg_settings
  WHERE name IN ('max_connections', 'effective_cache_size', 'work_mem');"

# 7. Check for long-running queries
docker exec -it postgres-persistent psql -U postgres -c "\
  SELECT
    pid,
    now() - pg_stat_activity.query_start AS duration,
    query,
    state,
    usename,
    application_name
  FROM pg_stat_activity
  WHERE (now() - pg_stat_activity.query_start) > interval '5 minutes'
    AND state != 'idle'
  ORDER BY duration DESC;"

# 8. Container health check
docker inspect postgres-persistent --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}'
docker exec postgres-persistent pg_isready -U postgres

# 9. Backup and maintenance (automated scripts)
# Create maintenance script: postgres-maintenance.ps1
# ====================================================
# docker exec postgres-persistent psql -U postgres -c "VACUUM ANALYZE;"
# docker exec postgres-persistent pg_dump -U app_user myappdb > "backups/myappdb_$(Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd_HHmmss').sql"

# 10. Resource monitoring script
# docker stats postgres-persistent --format "table {{.Container}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}\t{{.NetIO}}"

# Quick daily health check command
docker exec postgres-persistent psql -U postgres -c "\
  SELECT
    version(),
    current_database(),
    session_user,
    inet_server_addr(),
    inet_server_port(),
    pg_postmaster_start_time() AS server_start_time,
    current_timestamp - pg_postmaster_start_time() AS uptime;"
postgres.description: PostgreSQL daily management in Docker focuses on container lifecycle management (start/stop/restart), log monitoring, and database activity monitoring through psql commands. Docker provides container-level resource monitoring, while PostgreSQL system views offer database-specific insights. Container restart is much faster than SQL Server service restart. Docker logs provide comprehensive application and system messages. Multiple PostgreSQL containers can run independently. Container monitoring tools integrate with Docker ecosystem. Automated maintenance scripts can use Docker exec commands.

concepts.keyDifference: Container lifecycle vs Windows service management. docker logs vs Windows Event Log and SQL error log. Container resource stats vs Windows Performance Monitor. Per-container management vs system-wide SQL Server instance.

concepts.title: Development Workflow with Docker Compose

sqlServer.description: SQL Server development workflows typically use multiple databases on a single instance to represent different environments (Development, Testing, Staging). Each database has separate configurations, users, and data. Environment switching is managed through connection string changes in application configuration. Single SQL Server instance hosts multiple environment databases. Manual database creation and configuration for each environment. Backup/restore used to refresh test data from development. Connection strings manage environment switching.

postgres.code:
# PostgreSQL Development Workflow with Docker Compose
# Multi-environment setup with infrastructure as code

# 1. Create docker-compose.yml for development workflow
# =====================================================
version: '3.8'

services:
  # Development PostgreSQL
  postgres-dev:
    image: postgres:15
    container_name: myapp-postgres-dev
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp_dev
      POSTGRES_USER: dev_user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: DevPassword123!
      POSTGRES_INITDB_ARGS: "--encoding=UTF8 --locale=C"
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres-dev-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./init-scripts:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d
      - ./postgres-dev.conf:/etc/postgresql/postgresql.conf
    command: postgres -c config_file=/etc/postgresql/postgresql.conf
    networks:
      - myapp-network

  # Testing PostgreSQL
  postgres-test:
    image: postgres:15
    container_name: myapp-postgres-test
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp_test
      POSTGRES_USER: test_user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: TestPassword123!
    ports:
      - "5433:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres-test-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./init-scripts:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d
    tmpfs:
      - /var/lib/postgresql/data:rw,noexec,nosuid,size=1024m
    networks:
      - myapp-network

  # pgAdmin for all environments
  pgadmin:
    image: dpage/pgadmin4
    container_name: myapp-pgadmin
    environment:
      PGADMIN_DEFAULT_EMAIL: admin@myapp.com
      PGADMIN_DEFAULT_PASSWORD: AdminPassword123!
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
    volumes:
      - pgadmin-data:/var/lib/pgadmin
    networks:
      - myapp-network
    depends_on:
      - postgres-dev
      - postgres-test

  # Redis for caching (bonus)
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    container_name: myapp-redis
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    networks:
      - myapp-network

volumes:
  postgres-dev-data:
  postgres-test-data:
  pgadmin-data:

networks:
  myapp-network:
    driver: bridge

# 2. Environment-specific configuration files
# postgres-dev.conf (development optimized)
# ==========================================
# Development settings: verbose logging, development optimizations
log_statement = 'all'
log_min_duration_statement = 0
shared_buffers = 256MB
work_mem = 8MB
maintenance_work_mem = 128MB
checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9

# 3. Initialization scripts
# init-scripts/01-init-dev.sql (runs on first startup)
# ====================================================
-- Development database setup
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS app_schema;
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS app_schema.users (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Insert development test data
INSERT INTO app_schema.users (username, email) VALUES
('dev_user1', 'dev1@example.com'),
('dev_user2', 'dev2@example.com'),
('test_admin', 'admin@example.com');

# 4. Docker Compose workflow commands
# ===================================

# Start all services
docker-compose up -d

# Start specific environment
docker-compose up -d postgres-dev pgadmin

# View logs from all services
docker-compose logs -f

# View logs from specific service
docker-compose logs -f postgres-dev

# Stop all services
docker-compose down

# Stop and remove all data (DESTRUCTIVE)
docker-compose down -v

# Scale testing environment (multiple test instances)
docker-compose up -d --scale postgres-test=3

# 5. Development workflow scripts
# run-dev.ps1
# ===========
docker-compose up -d postgres-dev pgadmin
Write-Host "Development environment started:"
Write-Host "PostgreSQL Dev: localhost:5432"
Write-Host "pgAdmin: http://localhost:8080"
Write-Host "Username: dev_user, Password: DevPassword123!"

# run-tests.ps1
# =============
docker-compose up -d postgres-test
Write-Host "Running database tests..."
docker-compose exec postgres-test psql -U test_user -d myapp_test -c "\dt"
Write-Host "Test environment ready: localhost:5433"

# refresh-test-data.ps1
# ====================
Write-Host "Refreshing test data from development..."
docker-compose exec postgres-dev pg_dump -U dev_user myapp_dev > /tmp/dev_data.sql
docker-compose exec postgres-test psql -U test_user -d myapp_test < /tmp/dev_data.sql
Write-Host "Test data refreshed successfully"

# 6. Environment switching in application
# Connection strings for different environments
# Development: "Host=localhost;Port=5432;Database=myapp_dev;Username=dev_user;Password=DevPassword123!"
# Testing:     "Host=localhost;Port=5433;Database=myapp_test;Username=test_user;Password=TestPassword123!"
postgres.description: Docker Compose enables Infrastructure as Code for multi-environment PostgreSQL setups. Each environment runs in isolated containers with specific configurations, port mappings, and data volumes. The entire development infrastructure can be version-controlled and shared across team members. Complete development stack defined in version-controlled files. Each environment isolated with separate containers and networks. Easy to spin up/tear down environments for testing. Team members get identical development environments. Supports complex multi-service architectures (microservices).

concepts.keyDifference: Infrastructure as Code (YAML) vs manual database setup. Isolated containers vs shared SQL Server instance. Port-based environment separation vs database-based separation. Reproducible environments vs manual configuration management.

concepts.title: Hyper-V VM Alternative Setup

sqlServer.code:
-- SQL Server on Hyper-V Virtual Machine
-- Traditional virtualization approach for enterprise environments

-- 1. Hyper-V VM Specifications for SQL Server
-- Recommended VM configuration:
-- - CPU: 4-8 cores (depending on workload)
-- - RAM: 8-16GB (minimum 4GB)
-- - Storage: 100GB+ (separate disks for OS, data, logs, tempdb)
-- - Network: Dedicated network adapter

-- 2. VM Creation PowerShell Script (Run on Hyper-V host)
New-VM -Name "SQL-Server-VM" -MemoryStartupBytes 8GB -NewVHDPath "C:\Hyper-V\SQL-Server-VM.vhdx" -NewVHDSizeBytes 100GB
Set-VM -Name "SQL-Server-VM" -ProcessorCount 4
Add-VMNetworkAdapter -VMName "SQL-Server-VM" -SwitchName "Internal Network"
Start-VM -Name "SQL-Server-VM"

-- 3. Post-VM Setup (Inside the VM)
-- Install Windows Server 2019/2022
-- Configure static IP address
-- Join domain (if applicable)
-- Install SQL Server normally

-- 4. SQL Server Installation in VM (Same as physical)
-- Standard SQL Server installation process
-- Configure SQL Server for VM environment:

-- Set max server memory (leave RAM for OS)
EXEC sp_configure 'max server memory (MB)', 6144; -- 6GB if VM has 8GB RAM
RECONFIGURE;

-- Configure tempdb on separate drive
ALTER DATABASE tempdb MODIFY FILE
(NAME = tempdev, FILENAME = 'D:\TempDB\tempdb.mdf');
ALTER DATABASE tempdb MODIFY FILE
(NAME = templog, FILENAME = 'D:\TempDB\templog.ldf');

-- 5. VM-specific optimization
-- Enable lock pages in memory
-- Configure instant file initialization
-- Set up VM backup integration

-- 6. Network configuration for external access
-- Configure SQL Server for remote connections
EXEC sp_configure 'remote access', 1;
RECONFIGURE;

-- Enable TCP/IP protocol
-- Configure Windows Firewall
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SQL Server" -Direction Inbound -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 1433 -Action Allow

-- 7. VM Management
-- Snapshot before major changes
-- Regular VM backup
-- Monitor VM performance counters
sqlServer.description: SQL Server on Hyper-V follows traditional virtualization patterns where a complete Windows Server VM runs SQL Server as if on physical hardware. This approach provides maximum compatibility and isolation but requires significant resources and management overhead. Full Windows Server license required for each VM. Complete isolation between VMs. Traditional SQL Server management tools and processes. High resource overhead (OS + SQL Server per VM). Snapshot capability for development/testing.

postgres.code:
# PostgreSQL on Hyper-V Virtual Machine
# Enterprise-grade setup for traditional VM environments

# 1. Hyper-V VM Creation Script (PowerShell - Run as Administrator on Hyper-V host)
# =================================================================================

# Create new VM for PostgreSQL
New-VM -Name "PostgreSQL-Server" -MemoryStartupBytes 4GB -Generation 2 \
       -NewVHDPath "C:\Hyper-V\VMs\PostgreSQL-Server.vhdx" \
       -NewVHDSizeBytes 80GB -SwitchName "External Switch"

# Configure VM settings
Set-VM -Name "PostgreSQL-Server" -ProcessorCount 4 -DynamicMemory \
       -MemoryMinimumBytes 2GB -MemoryMaximumBytes 8GB

# Add additional data disk for PostgreSQL data
New-VHD -Path "C:\Hyper-V\VMs\PostgreSQL-Data.vhdx" -SizeBytes 100GB -Dynamic
Add-VMHardDiskDrive -VMName "PostgreSQL-Server" -Path "C:\Hyper-V\VMs\PostgreSQL-Data.vhdx"

# Configure network adapter
Set-VMNetworkAdapter -VMName "PostgreSQL-Server" -StaticMacAddress "00-15-5D-00-01-00"

# Enable secure boot and TPM (Generation 2 VM)
Set-VMFirmware -VMName "PostgreSQL-Server" -EnableSecureBoot On
Add-VMTpmChip -VMName "PostgreSQL-Server"

# Start VM
Start-VM -Name "PostgreSQL-Server"

# 2. VM Setup Script (Run inside Ubuntu Server VM after OS installation)
# =====================================================================

#!/bin/bash
# postgresql-vm-setup.sh

# Update system
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

# Install essential packages
sudo apt install -y curl wget gnupg2 software-properties-common apt-transport-https

# Add PostgreSQL official repository
wget --quiet -O - https://www.postgresql.org/media/keys/ACCC4CF8.asc | sudo apt-key add -
echo "deb http://apt.postgresql.org/pub/repos/apt/ $(lsb_release -cs)-pgdg main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pgdg.list

# Update package list
sudo apt update

# Install PostgreSQL 15
sudo apt install -y postgresql-15 postgresql-client-15 postgresql-contrib-15

# Configure PostgreSQL for VM environment
sudo systemctl stop postgresql

# Prepare data directory on separate disk
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/pgdata
sudo chown postgres:postgres /mnt/pgdata
sudo chmod 700 /mnt/pgdata

# Initialize new data directory
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/15/bin/initdb -D /mnt/pgdata --encoding=UTF8 --locale=en_US.UTF-8

# 3. PostgreSQL Configuration for VM (postgresql.conf)
# ===================================================

# Connection settings
listen_addresses = '*'
port = 5432
max_connections = 200

# Memory settings (for 4GB VM)
shared_buffers = 1GB
effective_cache_size = 3GB
work_mem = 16MB
maintenance_work_mem = 256MB

# Logging settings
log_destination = 'csvlog'
logging_collector = on
log_directory = 'log'
log_filename = 'postgresql-%Y-%m-%d_%H%M%S.log'
log_statement = 'mod'
log_min_duration_statement = 1000

# Performance settings
checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9
wal_buffers = 16MB
default_statistics_target = 100
random_page_cost = 1.1

# 4. Authentication Configuration (pg_hba.conf)
# ============================================

# Allow connections from network (adjust IP range as needed)
host    all             all             10.0.0.0/8              md5
host    all             all             192.168.0.0/16          md5

# 5. Create Database and Users
# ============================

sudo -u postgres psql << EOF
-- Create application database
CREATE DATABASE myappdb OWNER postgres;

-- Create application user
CREATE USER app_user WITH PASSWORD 'SecureVMPassword123!';
GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE myappdb TO app_user;

-- Create read-only user
CREATE USER readonly_user WITH PASSWORD 'ReadOnlyVMPassword123!';
GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE myappdb TO readonly_user;

-- Connect to application database and set permissions
\c myappdb

GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO app_user;
GRANT CREATE ON SCHEMA public TO app_user;
ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA public GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON TABLES TO app_user;

GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO readonly_user;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO readonly_user;
ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA public GRANT SELECT ON TABLES TO readonly_user;

EOF

# 6. System Service Configuration
# ==============================

# Update systemd service to use custom data directory
sudo systemctl edit postgresql
# Add:
[Service]
Environment="PGDATA=/mnt/pgdata"

# Enable and start PostgreSQL
sudo systemctl enable postgresql
sudo systemctl start postgresql

# 7. Firewall Configuration
# ========================

sudo ufw allow 5432/tcp
sudo ufw --force enable

# 8. VM Backup Script (scheduled via cron)
# ========================================

#!/bin/bash
# backup-postgresql-vm.sh

BACKUP_DIR="/backup/postgresql"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)

mkdir -p $BACKUP_DIR

# Database backup
sudo -u postgres pg_dump myappdb > "$BACKUP_DIR/myappdb_$DATE.sql"

# Configuration backup
sudo cp /etc/postgresql/15/main/postgresql.conf "$BACKUP_DIR/postgresql.conf_$DATE"
sudo cp /etc/postgresql/15/main/pg_hba.conf "$BACKUP_DIR/pg_hba.conf_$DATE"

# Cleanup old backups (keep 7 days)
find $BACKUP_DIR -name "*.sql" -mtime +7 -delete
find $BACKUP_DIR -name "*.conf_*" -mtime +7 -delete

# 9. VM Performance Monitoring
# ============================

# Install monitoring tools
sudo apt install -y htop iotop netcat-openbsd

# Create monitoring script
#!/bin/bash
# monitor-postgresql-vm.sh

echo "=== PostgreSQL VM Performance Monitor ==="
echo "Date: $(date)"
echo "Uptime: $(uptime)"
echo

echo "=== PostgreSQL Status ==="
sudo systemctl status postgresql --no-pager -l

echo "=== Active Connections ==="
sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT count(*) as active_connections FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active';"

echo "=== Database Sizes ==="
sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT datname, pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) as size FROM pg_database WHERE datname NOT IN ('template0', 'template1') ORDER BY pg_database_size(datname) DESC;"

echo "=== System Resources ==="
free -h
df -h /mnt/pgdata

# 10. Client Connection Examples
# =============================

# From Windows host (install PostgreSQL client tools)
psql -h 192.168.1.100 -p 5432 -U app_user -d myappdb

# From pgAdmin (add server with VM IP address)
# Host: 192.168.1.100
# Port: 5432
# Username: app_user
# Password: SecureVMPassword123!

# Connection string for applications:
# "Host=192.168.1.100;Port=5432;Database=myappdb;Username=app_user;Password=SecureVMPassword123!"
postgres.description: PostgreSQL on Hyper-V VM provides a traditional server-style deployment that's familiar to enterprise environments. The VM runs a complete Linux server with PostgreSQL installed normally, providing full control over the operating system, security policies, and resource allocation while maintaining enterprise-grade isolation. Provides complete OS control and traditional server management. Better suited for enterprise environments requiring VM isolation. Higher resource overhead but maximum compatibility. Familiar deployment model for traditional infrastructure teams. Supports enterprise backup/monitoring solutions designed for VMs.

concepts.keyDifference: Full Ubuntu Server VM vs lightweight container. Traditional service management (systemctl) vs container lifecycle. VM-level resource allocation vs container resource limits. Network-based access vs Docker port mapping.
